risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The ROADMAP study was a randomized, double‐blind, prevention study..." Comment: The report identifies the parent ROADMAP trial as randomized, but provides no direct description of how the allocation sequence was generated (e.g., computer-generated list, random numbers, blocks, stratification, central randomization). Because 'randomized' alone without further method details is insufficient for Low risk, and there is no evidence of a non-random method, the judgement is Unclear risk.

Quote: "The ROADMAP study was a randomized, double‐blind, prevention study" and "The ROADMAP study compared olmesartan medoxomil 40 mg o.d. versus placebo..." Comment: The report confirms randomization/double-blinding for the parent ROADMAP trial, but gives no explicit description of how allocation was concealed from the person enrolling participants (eg, central randomization, pharmacy-controlled allocation, or sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes). Because concealment mechanism is not reported, this domain is unclear by default.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The ROADMAP study was a randomized, double‐blind, prevention study with the primary endpoint ‘time to first event of microalbuminuria.’" Comment: This indicates participants and study personnel were blinded during the randomized ROADMAP trial. Although the report does not elaborate on how blinding was maintained, it directly states double-blinding for the parent randomized study from which these treatment groups derive. The dominant outcomes considered here (microalbuminuria by dipstick/UACR, blood pressure, renal function, cardiovascular events, mortality) are largely objective and not easily influenced by participants’ or treating personnel’s knowledge of allocation, so performance bias is unlikely to materially affect the overall study outcomes.

Quote: "Due to the observational character of the OFU only medical examinations and treatment according to standard medical care were documented." and "microalbuminuria was detected according to different local standards and was not centrally assessed. CV and renal events reported by the investigators were not adjudicated by an independent endpoint‐monitoring committee." and "New onset of diabetic retinopathy was diagnosed during the OFU in 23 patients (2.6%) of the former placebo group and in 8 patients (0.9%) of the former olmesartan group." Comment: Outcomes were assessed by local clinicians/investigators (clinician judgement), not by a blinded central assessor or independent adjudication committee. The OFU was observational after the blinded trial had ended, so treating physicians/investigators could know prior allocation. At least one outcome was subjective/judgement-dependent—new onset diabetic retinopathy diagnosis—so lack of blinded outcome assessment could bias measurement. Although some outcomes were more objective (e.g. lab values, mortality), by the worst-outcome rule the presence of unblinded clinician-assessed retinopathy makes this domain high risk.

Quote: "Out of 2198 eligible patients (placebo group 1104; olmesartan group: 1094) a total of 1758 patients (placebo group: [79.4%]; olmesartan group: [80.6%]) participated in the OFU." Quote: "In 862 (49.0%) OFU patients (436 [49.7%] of the former placebo group and 426 [48.4%] of the former olmesartan group) albumin excretion was measured at least once using albumin dipstick." Comment: The paper reports substantial post-randomization nonparticipation in the observational follow-up and only about half of OFU participants had albumin measurements, but it does not clearly report per-arm analysed denominators for each outcomes-of-interest/time point from the original randomized cohort, reasons for missing outcome data, or missing-data handling sufficient to judge whether attrition biased the randomized comparison. Because the information is incomplete for the analysed outcomes, this domain is best judged as unclear rather than high.
